subject: maths
title: Polynomials

nodes.title: Relate zeroes and coefficients of a polynomial
nodes.brief: The chapter's top skill: given a quadratic or cubic polynomial, find its zeroes and connect them to the coefficients via the sum/product relations (and the reverse тАФ build a polynomial from its zeroes).
nodes.quote: are the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial also related to its coefficients?
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Find zeroes and read off coefficient relations.
A zero of p(x) is a value k where p(k) = 0 (an x-intercept on the graph). For a quadratic ax┬▓+bx+c with zeroes alpha and beta: sum alpha+beta = -b/a and product alpha*beta = c/a. These two formulas give the link between every zero and the coefficients.

PART 2 тАФ Verify zeroes against the coefficient formulas.
Given the zeroes, compute their sum and product and check they equal -b/a and c/a. Example: 2x┬▓-5x+3 has zeroes 1 and 3/2; sum = 1+3/2 = 5/2 = -(-5)/2 тЬУ and product = 1*3/2 = 3/2 = 3/2 тЬУ.

PART 3 тАФ Reverse: build a polynomial from sum and product.
Given sum S and product P, the monic quadratic is x┬▓-Sx+P. For S=5, P=6: x┬▓-5x+6 = (x-2)(x-3), zeroes 2 and 3, confirming sum=5 and product=6 тЬУ.
keyMoves: State sum and product of zeroes in terms of coefficients
Verify or construct a polynomial from its zeroes
misconceptions: Thinking zeroes equal the coefficients themselves rather than being related by тИТb/a and c/a

nodes.title: Identify the degree of a polynomial
nodes.brief: Read off the highest power of the variable as the degree of the polynomial.
nodes.quote: the highest power of x in p(x) is called the degree of the polynomial p(x)
nodes.explanation: Degree is the highest exponent of the variable. 4x+2 has degree 1; 2y┬▓тИТ3y+4 has degree 2; the degree-6 example 7uтБ╢тИТ... has degree 6. This is the bedrock skill for classifying linear/quadratic/cubic.
keyMoves: Pick the highest exponent of the variable
misconceptions: Confusing the leading coefficient with the degree
Counting the number of terms instead of the highest power

nodes.title: Classify a polynomial as linear, quadratic, or cubic
nodes.brief: Name a polynomial by its degree: degree 1 linear, degree 2 quadratic, degree 3 cubic.
nodes.quote: A polynomial of degree 1 is called a linear polynomial.
nodes.explanation: Once the degree is known, the polynomial gets a name: degree 1 = linear, degree 2 = quadratic (general form ax┬▓+bx+c, aтЙа0), degree 3 = cubic (general form ax┬│+bx┬▓+cx+d, aтЙа0).
misconceptions: Calling 2x+5тИТx┬▓ linear because it starts with a linear term тАФ it is actually quadratic

nodes.title: Recognise expressions that are not polynomials
nodes.brief: Reject expressions with the variable in a denominator or under a root as non-polynomials.
nodes.quote: Expressions like 1/(x тИТ 1), тИЪx + 2, 1/(x┬▓ + 2x + 3) etc., are not polynomials.
nodes.explanation: A polynomial requires non-negative integer powers of the variable. Expressions with the variable in a denominator (1/(xтИТ1)) or under a radical (тИЪx+2) are not polynomials.
keyMoves: Check each term for a negative or fractional power of the variable
Reject if the variable appears in a denominator or under a root
misconceptions: Accepting тИЪx+2 as a polynomial because it looks simple

nodes.title: Evaluate a polynomial p(x) at x = k
nodes.brief: Substitute a number for the variable and compute the resulting value p(k).
nodes.quote: putting x = 2 in the polynomial, we get p(2) = 2┬▓ тИТ 3 ├Ч 2 тИТ 4 = тИТ 6
nodes.explanation: p(k) is found by replacing every x with k and evaluating. For p(x)=x┬▓тИТ3xтИТ4, p(2)=2┬▓тИТ3├Ч2тИТ4=тИТ6. This is the substitute-and-compute bedrock used to test zeroes.
keyMoves: Replace x by k in every term
Apply order of operations to compute the number
misconceptions: Mishandling signs when k is negative, e.g. (тИТ1)┬▓ becoming тИТ1

nodes.title: Evaluate a polynomial at a negative argument
nodes.brief: Substitute a negative number, correctly squaring/cubing the sign.
nodes.quote: p(тИТ1) = (тИТ1)┬▓ тИТ {3 ├Ч (тИТ1)} тИТ 4 = 1 + 3 тИТ 4 = 0
nodes.explanation: When k is negative, (тИТ1)┬▓ = 1 and the middle term тИТ3├Ч(тИТ1) = +3. Careful sign handling gives p(тИТ1)=0, showing тИТ1 is a zero. This generic signed-substitution skill is reused across the chapter (e.g. verifying cubic zeroes).
keyMoves: Track the sign of each term before summing
misconceptions: Writing (тИТ1)┬▓ = тИТ1
Dropping the double negative in тИТ3├Ч(тИТ1)

nodes.title: Define a zero of a polynomial
nodes.brief: A real number k is a zero of p(x) precisely when p(k)=0.
nodes.quote: a real number k is said to be a zero of a polynomial p(x), if p(k) = 0.
nodes.explanation: The defining property of a zero: substituting it makes the polynomial evaluate to 0. This is the criterion every zero-finding and zero-verifying step relies on.
misconceptions: Thinking a zero is where the polynomial equals the constant term rather than 0

nodes.title: Find the zero of a linear polynomial
nodes.brief: Set ax+b=0 and solve to get the single zero тИТb/a.
nodes.quote: the zero of the linear polynomial ax + b is тИТb/a = тИТ(Constant term)/(Coefficient of x).
nodes.explanation: A linear polynomial ax+b has exactly one zero, found by solving ax+b=0 тЗТ k=тИТb/a. Example: zero of 2x+3 is тИТ3/2. This is the first coefficient-to-zero relationship.
keyMoves: Solve the linear equation for x
misconceptions: Forgetting the negative sign: writing b/a instead of тИТb/a

nodes.title: Interpret a zero as an x-intercept of the graph
nodes.brief: Read the zeroes of p(x) as the x-coordinates where y=p(x) crosses the x-axis.
nodes.quote: the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial ax┬▓ + bx + c, a тЙа 0, are precisely the x-coordinates of the points where the parabola representing y = ax┬▓ + bx + c intersects the x-axis.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ The zeroтАУintercept connection.
Every zero of p(x) is the x-coordinate of a point where the graph y=p(x) meets the x-axis. At those points y=p(x)=0, which is exactly the definition of a zero тАФ graph language and algebra are describing the same thing.

PART 2 тАФ Apply the rule to a linear graph.
For y=2x+3 the line meets the x-axis when 2x+3=0, i.e. at x=тИТ3/2, giving the single crossing point (тИТ3/2, 0). So the only zero of 2x+3 is тИТ3/2 тАФ read directly from the x-axis crossing.

PART 3 тАФ Apply to a parabola and count correctly.
For y=x┬▓тИТ3xтИТ4 the parabola meets the x-axis at (тИТ1, 0) and (4, 0), so the two zeroes are тИТ1 and 4. Common traps: the y-intercept (where the curve crosses the y-axis) is not a zero; the vertex x-coordinate is not a zero unless the vertex sits on the x-axis.
keyMoves: Locate where the curve meets the x-axis
Read each x-coordinate as a zero
misconceptions: Reading the y-intercept instead of the x-intercept
Using the vertex x-value as a zero

nodes.title: Count the number of zeroes from a graph
nodes.brief: Count how many times the graph of y=p(x) meets the x-axis to get the number of zeroes.
nodes.quote: The number of zeroes is 2 as the graph intersects the x-axis at two points.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Count x-axis crossings, not other features.
The number of zeroes of y = p(x) equals the number of times the graph meets the x-axis. One x-intercept тЖТ 1 zero, two x-intercepts тЖТ 2 zeroes. Do not count turning points, the y-intercept, or any feature that is not an x-axis meeting point.

PART 2 тАФ Handle tangential touches carefully.
When the graph only touches (is tangent to) the x-axis without crossing, that point counts as one zero тАФ not two. A tangential touch is still an x-intercept; it just has multiplicity 2 algebraically, but there is still only one distinct zero at that x-value.
keyMoves: Count intersection points with the x-axis
Report that count as the number of zeroes
misconceptions: Counting turning points instead of x-axis crossings
Counting tangential touches as two zeroes

nodes.title: Bound the number of zeroes by the degree
nodes.brief: A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes.
nodes.quote: a polynomial p(x) of degree n has at most n zeroes.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ State the bounding rule: degree n gives at most n zeroes.
A polynomial of degree n has at most n real zeroes. A quadratic (degree 2) can have 0, 1, or 2 real zeroes; a cubic (degree 3) can have 1, 2, or 3. The degree is an upper bound, not the exact count.

PART 2 тАФ Apply the rule to specific polynomials.
For a polynomial of degree n, count zeroes by counting x-axis crossings on its graph тАФ there can be at most n. Example: x┬▓+1 has 0 real zeroes (graph never touches x-axis); (xтИТ1)┬▓ has 1; x┬▓тИТ5x+6 has 2. Never assume there are exactly n.
keyMoves: State the maximum number of zeroes as n
misconceptions: Assuming a degree-n polynomial always has exactly n real zeroes

nodes.title: Factorise a quadratic by splitting the middle term
nodes.brief: Split the middle term into two terms whose product equals (coeff of x┬▓)├Ч(constant)├Чx┬▓, then group.
nodes.quote: 2x┬▓ тИТ 8x + 6 = 2x┬▓ тИТ 6x тИТ 2x + 6 = 2x(x тИТ 3) тИТ 2(x тИТ 1)
nodes.explanation: To factorise ax┬▓+bx+c, split bx into two terms whose product is the a├Чc term (here 6├Ч2x┬▓=12x┬▓), then factor by grouping. 2x┬▓тИТ8x+6 splits as тИТ6xтИТ2x and factors to 2(xтИТ1)(xтИТ3).
keyMoves: Find two numbers with that product summing to b
misconceptions: Choosing split terms that sum to b but whose product is not a├Чc
Sign errors in the grouped factors

nodes.title: Factorise using the difference-of-squares identity
nodes.brief: Apply a┬▓тИТb┬▓=(aтИТb)(a+b) to factor a quadratic with no middle term.
nodes.quote: x┬▓ тИТ 3 = (x тИТ тИЪ3)(x + тИЪ3)
nodes.explanation: Example 3 uses a┬▓тИТb┬▓=(aтИТb)(a+b): x┬▓тИТ3 = x┬▓тИТ(тИЪ3)┬▓ = (xтИТтИЪ3)(x+тИЪ3), giving zeroes тИЪ3 and тИТтИЪ3. This is the identity-based factoring route for ax┬▓+c type quadratics.
misconceptions: Trying to factor x┬▓тИТ3 over integers and concluding it cannot be factored

nodes.title: Find zeroes from factored form via zero-product
nodes.brief: Set each linear factor equal to zero and solve to get the zeroes.
nodes.quote: the value of p(x) = 2x┬▓ тИТ 8x + 6 is zero when x тИТ 1 = 0 or x тИТ 3 = 0, i.e., when x = 1 or x = 3.
nodes.explanation: Once factored as (xтИТ1)(xтИТ3) (times a constant), the polynomial is zero exactly when a factor is zero. Each factor gives one zero. This is the zero-product step turning factors into zeroes.
misconceptions: Reading the zero as the constant inside the factor with the wrong sign (e.g. xтИТ3 giving тИТ3)

nodes.title: Verify the sum of zeroes equals тИТb/a
nodes.brief: Check that the sum of the two zeroes equals тИТ(coefficient of x)/(coefficient of x┬▓).
nodes.quote: Sum of its zeroes = 1 + 3 = 4 = тИТ(тИТ8)/2 = тИТ(Coefficient of x)/(Coefficient of x┬▓)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ State the sum formula and memorise the minus.
For a quadratic ax┬▓+bx+c with zeroes ╬▒ and ╬▓, the sum is ╬▒+╬▓ = тИТb/a. The minus on b is the most common error: writing b/a instead of тИТb/a gives the wrong sign. Remember: sum gets the MINUS; product does not.

PART 2 тАФ Compute the sum and тИТb/a from the coefficients.
Add the two zeroes. For 2x┬▓тИТ8x+6 with zeroes 1 and 3: ╬▒+╬▓ = 1+3 = 4. Also тИТb/a = тИТ(тИТ8)/2 = 8/2 = 4. They match тЬУ.

PART 3 тАФ Verify and flag the two most common errors.
If ╬▒+╬▓ = тИТb/a the sum relation is verified. Common errors: (1) using b/a without the minus тЖТ gets тИТ4 instead of 4; (2) dividing by the constant c instead of by the leading coefficient a.
misconceptions: Forgetting the leading negative sign in тИТb/a
Dividing by the constant instead of by a

nodes.title: Verify the product of zeroes equals c/a
nodes.brief: Check that the product of the two zeroes equals (constant term)/(coefficient of x┬▓).
nodes.quote: Product of its zeroes = 1 ├Ч 3 = 3 = 6/2 = (Constant term)/(Coefficient of x┬▓)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Identify the product of zeroes formula.
For a quadratic ax┬▓+bx+c with zeroes ╬▒ and ╬▓, the product is ╬▒╬▓ = c/a. Note: no minus sign тАФ only the sum relation carries the negative (╬▒+╬▓ = тИТb/a). Confusing the two is the most common sign error on this topic.

PART 2 тАФ Compute the product and the ratio c/a.
Multiply the two zeroes and compute c/a from the coefficients. For 2x┬▓тИТ8x+6 with zeroes 1 and 3: ╬▒╬▓ = 1├Ч3 = 3; c/a = 6/2 = 3. They match тЬУ.

PART 3 тАФ State the verification.
If ╬▒╬▓ = c/a the product relation is verified. If they do not match, recheck the zeroes or the coefficient read-off тАФ do not add a minus sign to 'fix' the mismatch.
misconceptions: Putting a negative sign on c/a (only the sum carries the negative)

nodes.title: State the general sum/product relations for a quadratic
nodes.brief: Recall ╬▒+╬▓=тИТb/a and ╬▒╬▓=c/a for zeroes of ax┬▓+bx+c.
nodes.quote: sum of zeroes = ╬▒ + ╬▓ = тИТb/a = тИТ(Coefficient of x)/(Coefficient of x┬▓),
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ State both formulas for the general quadratic ax┬▓+bx+c.
If ╬▒, ╬▓ are the two zeroes of ax┬▓+bx+c (aтЙа0), then sum ╬▒+╬▓ = тИТb/a and product ╬▒╬▓ = c/a. The negative sign belongs to the sum formula, not the product formula.

PART 2 тАФ Identify a, b, c correctly from the polynomial.
Write the polynomial in standard form before reading off a, b, c. For x┬▓тИТ5x+6: a=1, b=тИТ5, c=6; so ╬▒+╬▓ = тИТ(тИТ5)/1 = 5 and ╬▒╬▓ = 6/1 = 6. A missing term means the coefficient is 0.
misconceptions: Swapping which relation gets the negative sign

nodes.title: Build a quadratic from given sum and product of zeroes
nodes.brief: Use ╬▒+╬▓=тИТb/a, ╬▒╬▓=c/a with a=1 to write a quadratic with the given sum and product.
nodes.quote: So, one quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is x┬▓ + 3x + 2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ The monic form: x┬▓тИТSx+P.
If the zeroes have sum S and product P, the simplest (a=1) quadratic is x┬▓тИТSx+P. The coefficient of x is тИТS (negative of the sum) and the constant term is P.

PART 2 тАФ Apply: sum=тИТ3, product=2.
b = тИТ(sum) = тИТ(тИТ3) = 3 and c = product = 2, giving x┬▓+3x+2. Check: zeroes are тИТ1 and тИТ2; sum = тИТ1+(тИТ2) = тИТ3 тЬУ and product = (тИТ1)(тИТ2) = 2 тЬУ. Any non-zero scalar multiple k┬╖(x┬▓+3x+2) also has the same zeroes.
misconceptions: Using b=sum instead of b=тИТsum
Forgetting the polynomial form is x┬▓тИТ(sum)x+(product)

nodes.title: Verify a value is a zero of a cubic by substitution
nodes.brief: Substitute a candidate into the cubic and confirm the result is 0.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Substitute the candidate value into every term.
Replace every x with the candidate. For p(x) = ax┬│+bx┬▓+cx+d, write a(candidate)┬│ + b(candidate)┬▓ + c(candidate) + d without evaluating yet тАФ write out the full substitution first so no term is accidentally dropped.

PART 2 тАФ Evaluate each term carefully, then sum.
Compute each term in order. For a negative candidate, keep track of the sign: (тИТk)┬│ = тИТk┬│, (тИТk)┬▓ = +k┬▓. For a fractional candidate keep everything as fractions throughout. Sum the four results.

PART 3 тАФ Confirm the total is 0.
If the sum equals 0 the candidate is a zero. If not, the candidate is not a zero тАФ recheck each term for a slip rather than concluding the candidate is correct.
keyMoves: Substitute the candidate into the cubic
misconceptions: Arithmetic slips computing higher powers
Mishandling fractional or negative arguments

nodes.title: State the sum/pair-sum/product relations for a cubic
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Identify the four coefficients.
For ax┬│ + bx┬▓ + cx + d, read off a (the leading coefficient), b (the x┬▓ coefficient), c (the x coefficient), and d (the constant term). Be careful with embedded signs: in 2x┬│ тИТ 5x┬▓ + 7x тИТ 3 the values are a=2, b=тИТ5, c=7, d=тИТ3 тАФ do not strip the minus signs.

PART 2 тАФ Write the three symmetric relations and remember the signs.
With zeroes ╬▒, ╬▓, ╬│: sum ╬▒+╬▓+╬│ = тИТb/a (minus); pair-sum ╬▒╬▓+╬▓╬│+╬│╬▒ = c/a (no minus); product ╬▒╬▓╬│ = тИТd/a (minus again). Memory aid: minus on sum and product, no minus on pair-sum.
misconceptions: Dropping the negative on ╬▒╬▓╬│=тИТd/a
Putting a negative on the pair-sum c/a

nodes.title: Verify the cubic zeroтАУcoefficient relations numerically
nodes.brief: Compute the sum, pair-sum, and product of the three zeroes and match them to тИТb/a, c/a, тИТd/a.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 тАФ Add the three zeroes to verify the sum relation.
For ax┬│+bx┬▓+cx+d with zeroes ╬▒, ╬▓, ╬│, compute ╬▒+╬▓+╬│ and check it equals тИТb/a. For 3x┬│тИТ5x┬▓тИТ11xтИТ3 (a=3, b=тИТ5) with zeroes 3, тИТ1, тИТ1/3: sum = 3+(тИТ1)+(тИТ1/3) = 2тИТ1/3 = 5/3 = тИТ(тИТ5)/3 тЬУ.

PART 2 тАФ Compute all three pairwise products to verify the pair-sum.
Form ╬▒╬▓+╬▓╬│+╬│╬▒ and check it equals c/a (no minus sign). ╬▒╬▓=3├Ч(тИТ1)=тИТ3; ╬▓╬│=(тИТ1)├Ч(тИТ1/3)=1/3; ╬│╬▒=(тИТ1/3)├Ч3=тИТ1. Sum = тИТ3+1/3тИТ1 = тИТ4+1/3 = тИТ12/3+1/3 = тИТ11/3 = (тИТ11)/3 тЬУ.

PART 3 тАФ Multiply all three zeroes to verify the product relation.
Compute ╬▒╬▓╬│ and check it equals тИТd/a (has minus sign). ╬▒╬▓╬│ = 3├Ч(тИТ1)├Ч(тИТ1/3) = 1 = тИТ(тИТ3)/3 тЬУ. Memory aid: sum and product carry the minus (тИТb/a and тИТd/a); pair-sum does not (c/a).
keyMoves: Add the three zeroes for the sum
Match each to the coefficient ratio
misconceptions: Forgetting one of the three pairwise products
Sign errors with fractional zeroes

narrative:
## What I read (review pass)

I re-read the full segmented transcription of NCERT Class 10 Maths chapter jemh102 "Polynomials" (`extractions/_text/jemh102.txt`, 6 SEGs: 2.1 Introduction, 2.2 Geometrical Meaning of Zeroes, EXERCISE 2.1, 2.3 Relationship between Zeroes and Coefficients, EXERCISE 2.2, 2.4 Summary) and the shared CBSE Maths SQP + Marking Scheme (`_maths-exams.txt`, Standard paper Q1 and Q8 with answer keys).

Scope note: this transcription does NOT contain a Division Algorithm section тАФ the chapter ends at 2.3/Summary even though SEG 0's intro line mentions it. Recorded in absentNotes; no nodes invented for it.

## Corrections applied to the draft

1. GOAL CITATION (D-120 fix). The draft cited the goal node `relate-zeroes-and-coefficients-of-polynomials` at sec=3 with the quote "In this chapter, we will try to answer these questions. We will also study the division algorithm for polynomials." That line lives in SEG 0 (not SEG 3) AND it foregrounds the division algorithm, which the chapter never delivers. Re-quoted to the SEG-0 line that actually states the chapter's goal тАФ "are the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial also related to its coefficients?" тАФ and fixed sec to 0. This is a verbatim substring of SEG 0.

2. EDGE FIX тАФ second root removed. `bound-number-of-zeroes-by-degree` appeared only as a parent (of `count-zeroes-from-graph` and `identify-degree-of-polynomial`) and never as a child, so the graph had TWO roots. Added a true prerequisite edge `relate-zeroes-and-coefficients-of-polynomials тЖТ bound-number-of-zeroes-by-degree` (knowing how many zeroes a polynomial can have is a genuine prerequisite to relating those zeroes to coefficients). Now a single root with no orphans.

3. GATE srcLabel cleanup. Two CAS/numeric gates carried the placeholder srcLabel "2.3 (worked example p.x)" тАФ the page-range marker was an artifact. Renamed to "2.3 (worked example 2x┬▓ тИТ 8x + 6)" so the locator is meaningful; the verbatim quote is unchanged and remains the authoritative citation.

## Verification performed

- CITATIONS: every node quote re-checked as a verbatim substring of its SEG. All 21 verify (including the multi-line cubic-relations quote at lines 236-238 and the signed-substitution line at 21).
- GATES: 21 gates, one per node, 100% coverage. Every gate quote re-checked verbatim тАФ chapter gates against jemh102 SEGs, the two exam gates against `_maths-exams.txt` Marking Scheme lines "1. D) -6,6" and "8. B) 8ЁЭСе2- 20" (copied with the OCR's literal mathematical-bold "ЁЭСе"). Each grader/expected/answer is self-consistent for the deterministic tester (numeric values match, mcq `correct` is among `options`, CAS forms are single-variable in x).
- EDGES: acyclic (all edges flow from higher skill toward bedrock; no back-edges), single root, no orphan node, every endpoint is a defined slug.
- GRAIN: each node is one failable step. Example 5's substitution check (`verify-cubic-zero-by-substitution`) is kept separate from the relations check (`verify-cubic-zero-coefficient-relations`), per one-failable-thing-one-node. No pure-exposition node (history of the word "quadratic", the parabola-shape cases, the no-zero case) was promoted to a skill.

## What was absent / uncertain

- Division Algorithm, dividing-out a known factor, hand-plotting graphs (explicitly excluded by the chapter footnote), and the formal proof of the cubic relations are all genuinely absent тАФ recorded in absentNotes, not failed.
- Goal gate Q8: the OCR garbles the zeroes ("тИТ тИЪ5 / 2 and тИЪ5 / 2"); the keyed answer 8x┬▓тИТ20 is taken as canonical from the Marking Scheme regardless of the arithmetic ambiguity, since the gate is a deterministic MCQ against the published key.
absentNotes: This chapter transcription teaches ONLY: classifying polynomials by degree, evaluating polynomials / defining zeroes, the geometric meaning of zeroes (reading and counting them from graphs), and the zeroтАУcoefficient relationships for quadratics and cubics (both verifying them and constructing a polynomial from a given sum and product). A fuller treatment would also cover, but THIS transcription does NOT teach (so these are omitted, not failed): (1) the Division Algorithm for Polynomials тАФ polynomial long division and p(x)=g(x)├Чq(x)+r(x) тАФ despite SEG 0's intro line promising it, the section is absent here; (2) finding all zeroes of a cubic/quartic given two known zeroes by dividing out the known quadratic factor; (3) plotting/sketching parabolas and cubics by hand (explicitly excluded by the chapter footnote: "Plotting of graphs of quadratic or cubic polynomials is not meant to be done by the students, nor is to be evaluated"); (4) the formal proof/derivation of the cubic relations (stated as "it can be proved" without proof); (5) forming a new polynomial from transformed zeroes such as ╬▒┬▓, ╬▓┬▓ (this appears in CBSE SQP problems but the chapter text itself never demonstrates it, so no node тАФ only its prerequisite sum/product skills are taught here).
